EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved into Utah beginning November 8, bringing gusty winds, heavy snow, and even a funnel cloud to the state. The first wave brought the winds and a burst of heavy, wet snow, while the majority of the snowfall along the Wasatch Front occurred after colder air moved into the area, producing widespread lake effect snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals for the southern Wasatch Front in this 3-day event included 12 inches of snow in Springville, 12 inches in Suncrest, and 11 inches of snow in Pleasant Grove. In addition, significant tree damage occurred across the area, with the worst of the damage in the Provo area. Several of these downed tree limbs hit power lines, causing temporary power outages across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved into Utah beginning November 8, bringing gusty winds, heavy snow, and even a funnel cloud to the state. The first wave brought the winds and a burst of heavy, wet snow, while the majority of the snowfall along the Wasatch Front occurred after colder air moved into the area, producing widespread lake effect snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cold front brought a first round of snowfall to the northern Wasatch Front on November 9, followed by significant lake effect snow accumulations on both November 10 and November 11. Storm total snowfall included 31.5 inches of snow at Bountiful Bench, 22 inches in Centerville, 20 inches at the North Salt Lake Bench, 16 inches in Bountiful, and 12 inches of snow in Fruit Heights. Some minor tree damage occurred due to the wet, heavy snow at the beginning of the event, but damage was less significant than further south along the Wasatch Front. This was partially due to a previous storm in October that had downed many of the weaker tree branches in the northern Wasatch Front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved into Utah beginning November 8, bringing gusty winds, heavy snow, and even a funnel cloud to the state. The first wave brought the winds and a burst of heavy, wet snow, while the majority of the snowfall along the Wasatch Front occurred after colder air moved into the area, producing widespread lake effect snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys received a first round of snowfall on November 9, followed by significant lake effect snow accumulations both November 10 and 11. Storm totals included 27.5 inches of snow at Upper Millcreek, 25 inches at Cottonwood Heights, 23 inches in Sandy, 21 inches in the Upper Avenues, 15 inches at the Salt Lake International Airport, and 11 inches of snow in Tooele. The wet, heavy snowfall on November 9 caused widespread damage to tree branches, many of which still had leaves in place from the warm autumn. Several of these downed branches also caused power outages across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, cold storm system moved through Utah at the end of February and the beginning of March, with the initial cold front entering the state on February 29th. This storm brought widespread heavy snowfall to the northern half of Utah. Note that this event began in February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals across west central Utah included 7 inches of new snow in Oak City and Delta, as well as 6 inches of snow in Fillmore. Note that this event began in February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front brought gusty winds and widespread areas of snow to Utah. The strongest winds occurred along and just behind the front, especially across the west deserts and the favored areas of southwest Utah. The heaviest snow fell over the Tooele Valley and western potions of the Salt Lake Valley, as lake-effect snow squalls developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong winds combined to create hazardous conditions across southwest Utah. Areas near Cedar City received a storm total of 5 inches new snow, and the Milford ASOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 60 mph as the cold front passed through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved into Utah beginning November 8, bringing gusty winds, heavy snow, and even a funnel cloud to the state. The first wave brought the winds and a burst of heavy, wet snow, while the majority of the snowfall along the Wasatch Front occurred after colder air moved into the area, producing widespread lake effect snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 74 mph at Vernon Hill and the Great Salt Lake Marina, 61 mph at Flight Park South, and 58 mph at the I-80 at Milepost 78 sensor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wasatch Mountains south of Interstate 80 received heavy snowfall from the initial cold front, and from the subsequent lake effect snowfall, leading to high snow amounts across the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, moist, and slow-moving Pacific storm system entered Utah on March 17th, with the impacts of the storm lingering into March 20th. The storm brought strong, gusty winds to many of the valley locations across Utah on May 17th, then brought heavy snow to Utah's mountains the 18th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the Wasatch Mountains south of Interstate 80 were impressive, with storm totals of 50 inches of snow at Alta Ski Lifts, 45 inches at the crest of Brighton Resort, 37 inches at Sundance Resort, 35 inches at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort, 29 inches at Provo Canyon, 27 inches at Canyons, and 25 inches at Solitude Mountain Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front swept through Utah on March 26th, initiating showers and strong, gusty winds. These winds were widespread across northern Utah on the morning of the 26th, and even caused damage in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 75 mph at Flight Park South, 69 mph at Vernon Hill and the Great Salt Lake Marina, 63 mph at Bluffdale, 59 mph at Riverton, and 58 mph at Olympus Cove. These winds caused damage at a shopping center in Draper, blowing out two large windows. In addition, a semitrailer was blown over by the strong winds near Point of the Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms brought significant snow accumulations to the La Sal Mountains since early February. The final storm prior to the deadly avalanche deposited over 2 feet of new snow on northwest aspects near the accident site and created conditions favorable for avalanches as a soft slab snow layer was overlaid onto a hardened weak surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of four snowmobile riders were heading into the Beaver Basin area and noticed the snow pack becoming increasingly unstable by the late morning hours. The group found a turn-around point in a nearby meadow which was in the avalanche zone. The last rider heading to the meadow turn-around point was caught by a massive avalanche which released higher up on the mountain.  The victim and his snowmobile were buried under about twelve feet of snow, and were not able to be extricated until the next day.  The top of the avalanche path was near the 11,200 foot level and traveled down about 1250 vertical feet with a path width of up to 800 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional mid-level moisture moved into Utah in mid-May, helping to initiate Utah's first severe thunderstorms of 2012. In the first few days of the event, these were largely high-based thunderstorms producing gusty winds. A Pacific storm system then moved into the area on May 18th, primarily producing widespread rain in northern Utah. However, this storm also created strong downslope winds in the Uinta Basin, and helped to initiate thunderstorms in southern Utah capable of producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Causeway sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well below normal snow pack over the northeast Utah mountains and persistent below normal precipitation through the spring and early summer months allowed the drought to continue strengthen and expand across eastern Utah through June. High wildland fire danger, agricultural impacts and water shortages and restrictions were all being realized by the end of this month across many portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted across the western portion of the zone while extreme drought conditions expanded into the easther portion as precipitation through the spring and early summer months remained well below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted across Utah through the end of June. This led to several more wildfire ignitions at the end of the month, some of which caused property damage or injuries. Note that most of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New Harmony Fire started on June 27th near New Harmony, Utah, due to a water pump malfunction on private land. The fire burned 1,957 acres before it was 100% contained on June 30th. 7 residences were destroyed, 22 non-residential buildings were destroyed, and numerous vehicles were heavily damaged. At least 60 power poles were damaged in the fire, and there was one injury, a firefighter who received first and second degree burns. In addition to physical damages, fire suppression costs totaled $421,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted across Utah through the end of June. This led to several more wildfire ignitions at the end of the month, some of which caused property damage or injuries. Note that most of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clay Springs Fire began on July 27th east of Delta, Utah, and was human-caused. Clay Springs was the largest fire of the year in the state of Utah, burning a total of 107,846 acres before it was 100% contained on July 21st. The fire forced the evacuation of nearly 1,000 people, including the entire communities of Oak City and Fool Creek. Damages from the fire included one cabin that was destroyed, minor damage to a few other residences, 3 city storage structures destroyed, and dozens of power poles damaged. Two firefighters were injured by the blaze. In addition to physical damages, fire suppression cost a total of $6.6 million. Note that this event continued into July, and monetary property damages will be listed on the July report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted across Utah through the end of June. This led to several more wildfire ignitions at the end of the month, some of which caused property damage or injuries. Note that most of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rose Crest Fire began on July 29th in Herriman, Utah. The fire was started when a car was parked on a very dry field; the heat from the car ignited the grass, and the fire quickly spread into the residential neighborhood. The fire forced the evacuation of nearly 1,000 residences, and eventually burned 612 acres before it was 100% contained on July 2nd. 4 residences were destroyed, 8 other residences sustained moderate to heavy damage, and 30 non-residential buildings were destroyed. In addition to these property damages, the fire suppression cost a total of $540,000. Note that this event continued into July, and monetary property damages will be listed on the July report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well below normal snow pack over the northeast Utah mountains and persistent below normal precipitation through the spring and early summer months allowed the drought to continue strengthen and expand across eastern Utah through June. High wildland fire danger, agricultural impacts and water shortages and restrictions were all being realized by the end of this month across many portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions expanded across east central Utah as precipitation through the spring and early summer months remained well below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unsettled air mass brought rainfall rates as high as 1.5 inches per hour over portions of southeast Utah during the afternoon of September 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in a flow of mud and water across Highway 191 approximately 8 miles north of Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe drought conditions remained in place across eastern Utah through July. Rainfall amounts began to increase in coverage and intensity through the month with the start of the monsoon, however the majority of the reporting stations across the region ended July with below normal precipitation readings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions remain in place across the region as precipitation totals continue to run below normal through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the seasons transitioned from fall to winter, the storm track became more favorable for precipitation across the eastern Utah, especially during the latter half of the month. As a result, near normal precipitation was reported at many climate stations in eastern Utah for the first time in many months. However, the precipitation was not enough to ease drought conditions and little change was seen in the severe to extreme categories in place across eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions remained unchanged across the eastern Uinta Mountains through the month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall moved across the northern Salt Wash basin during the overnight hours of October 12th. The runoff from these storms reached the Delicate Arch area of Arches National Park on October 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding along the Salt Wash inundated the road to Wolf Ranch and closed it for several hours.  Many vehicles and tourists were trapped in the Delicate Arch parking area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Park Service personnel reported flash flooding along Upheaval Wash. The fast moving flood waters cut into the steep banks which damaged the White Rim Road and made it impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep Pacific moisture, combined with embedded weather disturbances moving through the region in the middle of January, resulted in heavy and wet snowfall in the mountains and mountain valleys of northern Utah. Occasional heavy rain was observed in the northern Utah valleys. This rain, when combined with frozen ground and snowmelt, produced flooding in portions of Cache Valley and Ogden Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on frozen ground, combined with some snowmelt, led to the flooding of some residential basements in Liberty, Utah. This flooding was eventually mitigated with pumps and sandbags.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system moved through Utah on January 21st and 22nd. Gusty winds were observed both ahead of and behind the strong cold front. Flooding, again due to heavy rain on top of frozen ground, was observed in the Wasatch mountain valleys. Heavy snowfall was also observed in the higher elevations across much of Utah, as well as the benches of the northern Wasatch Front behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding in and around the town of Morgan, Utah, with the most damage occurring along Morgan Valley Drive. Four residential basements were flooded in this area, and water flowed over some roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large avalanche was triggered in the backcountry near Big Cottonwood Canyon, resulting in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three men, a skier and two split-boarders, went up Big Cottonwood Canyon on the morning of January 28th. They eventually reached Kessler Peak, and began to descend the west face, with one of the split-boarders in the lead. The man in the lead triggered a hard slab avalanche, which averaged 3 feet deep and nearly 1,000 feet wide.  The avalanche ran 2,500 feet vertically. The man was buried in the avalanche, and the other two friends, who were above the avalanche path, began attempts to locate him. They eventually found him and dug him out, but he died on the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another downslope windstorm impacted the northern Wasatch Front, bringing gusty winds to the Farmington area. However, these winds were significantly weaker than the windstorm a couple of months prior, in December 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two different sensors in the Farmington area recorded strong downslope wind gusts, with a maximum recorded gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system moved through Utah on January 21st and 22nd. Gusty winds were observed both ahead of and behind the strong cold front. Flooding, again due to heavy rain on top of frozen ground, was observed in the Wasatch mountain valleys. Heavy snowfall was also observed in the higher elevations across much of Utah, as well as the benches of the northern Wasatch Front behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Creek SNOTEL received 2.20 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 22 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system moved through Utah on January 21st and 22nd. Gusty winds were observed both ahead of and behind the strong cold front. Flooding, again due to heavy rain on top of frozen ground, was observed in the Wasatch mountain valleys. Heavy snowfall was also observed in the higher elevations across much of Utah, as well as the benches of the northern Wasatch Front behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall across the northern Wasatch Front included 9 inches at Bountiful Bench, 7 inches in Bountiful and South Ogden, and 6 inches at Northeast Ogden Bench and Brigham City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler triggered an avalanche in the mountains of Central Utah. Two people were initially caught in the avalanche, but there was only one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four snowmobilers were finishing up a day of riding west of Lost Creek Reservoir. They were well aware of the avalanche danger, and as such, had avoided climbing any hills through the day. However, wanting to get back home quicker, they cut across one of the hills, and an avalanche was triggered. One rider was quickly caught in the slide, but was able to stay on his sled, and grabbed the throttle, which propelled him out of the slide area. A second rider was caught and fully buried in the snow. After more than an hour of searching by his fellow riders, he was eventually found and dug out, but he had already passed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying cold front moved through west central Utah beginning early on the morning of February 19th, bringing locally heavy snow to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell through the entire day of February 19th, with the heaviest burst of snow occurring along the cold front in the early morning hours. Fillmore reported 8 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving through Utah brought strong winds to the northern half of the state, especially along the higher terrain. These winds added to already dangerous avalanche conditions, and there was one avalanche fatality during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Snowbasin-Straw Top sensor recorded strong winds for several hours, with a maximum wind gust of 89 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving through Utah brought strong winds to the northern half of the state, especially along the higher terrain. These winds added to already dangerous avalanche conditions, and there was one avalanche fatality during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two snowboarders were in Dutch Draw, a backcountry area south of the Canyons Resort. After a brief stop, the victim went ahead of the other snowboarder, where he triggered an avalanche, was caught, and was buried. While he was eventually dug out of the snow by his companion,  and other snowboarders in the area, he was buried for approximately 40 minutes. Attempts at resuscitation were unsuccessful, and he died at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep Pacific moisture, combined with embedded weather disturbances moving through the region in the middle of January, resulted in heavy and wet snowfall in the mountains and mountain valleys of northern Utah. Occasional heavy rain was observed in the northern Utah valleys. This rain, when combined with frozen ground and snowmelt, produced flooding in portions of Cache Valley and Ogden Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eden received 11 inches of new snow, while Liberty received 10 inches of snow. The bulk of this snow occurred during the afternoon and evening of January 18th, with periods of snow or a mix of rain and snow occurring periodically through the 19th and 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent and moist westerly flow with embedded disturbances resulted in heavy snow for the mountains and significant snowfall accumulations for some lower elevation aras of esatern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 8 to 14 inches were measured across the area. Additionally, wind gusts of 30 to 60 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow with localized blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system moved through Utah on January 21st and 22nd. Gusty winds were observed both ahead of and behind the strong cold front. Flooding, again due to heavy rain on top of frozen ground, was observed in the Wasatch mountain valleys. Heavy snowfall was also observed in the higher elevations across much of Utah, as well as the benches of the northern Wasatch Front behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed across the Salt Lake and Tooele valleys on the morning of January 21st, then rapidly decreased when precipitation began. Maximum wind gusts included 72 mph at the Flight Park South sensor near Point of the Mountain, 68 mph at South Mountain, 66 mph at Stockton Bar, 62 mph at Vernon Hill, and 59 mph at the Court H sensor near Tooele.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system moved through Utah on January 21st and 22nd. Gusty winds were observed both ahead of and behind the strong cold front. Flooding, again due to heavy rain on top of frozen ground, was observed in the Wasatch mountain valleys. Heavy snowfall was also observed in the higher elevations across much of Utah, as well as the benches of the northern Wasatch Front behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum wind gusts in West Central Utah included 73 mph at the US-6 at Eureka sensor and 63 mph at the Delta Bus Garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm system moved through Utah on December 16, bringing a shot of snow to northern Utah. This was quickly followed by a deeper and colder Pacific system on the 17th, which produced both gusty winds and heavy snowfall across much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hans Flat Ranger Station reported 8 inches of new snow for the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm system moved into Utah beginning late on December 23, with the heaviest snow falling on December 24. The largest snow totals were observed in far northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals across the area included 21 inches of snow at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort, 18 inches of snow at Alta Ski Lifts, and 15 inches of snow at Canyons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, cold storm system moved through Utah at the end of February and the beginning of March, with the initial cold front entering the state on February 29th. This storm brought widespread heavy snowfall to the northern half of Utah. Note that this event continued into March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals across the area included 43 inches at Alta Ski Lifts, 40 inches at Brighton Resort, 27 inches at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort, and 24 inches at Provo Canyon. Note that this event continued into March.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system moved through Utah on January 21st and 22nd. Gusty winds were observed both ahead of and behind the strong cold front. Flooding, again due to heavy rain on top of frozen ground, was observed in the Wasatch mountain valleys. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture interacted with a strong upper-level low on September 11th, producing heavy rain and flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. The most significant event of the day was the failure of the Laub Dike in Santa Clara, causing flooding in many area homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in multiple locations across Zion National Park, including in the Narrows. The North Fork of the Virgin River rose from 52 cfs to 1004 cfs in one hour. Large waterfalls coming off of cliffs closed numerous hikes and temporarily stranded hikers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture interacted with a strong upper-level low on September 11th, producing heavy rain and flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. The most significant event of the day was the failure of the Laub Dike in Santa Clara, causing flooding in many area homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported over the bridge where old US Highway 91 crosses the Santa Clara River. This flooding caused the road to be closed for a few hours in the late morning and early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture that moved into Utah at the end of August remained in place for the beginning of September. This moist airmass produced widespread showers and thunderstorms, with several of them becoming severe or producing flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers at Capitol Reef National Park reported flash flooding along both Sulphur Creek and Sheets Gulch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over Capitol Reef National Park on August 10th, causing flash flooding in multiple parts of the park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported by the park rangers at Capitol Reef National Park. This flooding primarily occurred along the Scenic Drive, closing the Grand Wash and Capitol Gorge areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms continued across Utah in the middle of August. The strongest of these thunderstorms most commonly produced flash flooding in southern Utah, but isolated occurrences of severe winds and a tornado were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milford ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms continued across Utah in the middle of August. The strongest of these thunderstorms most commonly produced flash flooding in southern Utah, but isolated occurrences of severe winds and a tornado were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beaver County Sheriff reported a tornado along the Utah/Nevada border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across southern Utah once again reached a peak on August 22nd through 24th, with multiple flash floods reported each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Sulpher Creek near Capitol Reef National Park, with the leading wave of the flood reaching 4 feet in height.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across southern Utah once again reached a peak on August 22nd through 24th, with multiple flash floods reported each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported by park rangers in the Buckskin Gulch area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms continued across Utah in the middle of August. The strongest of these thunderstorms most commonly produced flash flooding in southern Utah, but isolated occurrences of severe winds and a tornado were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding occurred in the cities of Enoch and Cedar City on the afternoon of August 17th. Enoch had the most significant damage, with 110 houses damaged by flood waters, while Cedar City mostly experienced street flooding. About 30 of those 110 houses experienced major damage, typically over $30,000 per house, while the rest had more minor damage. Flood waters and some sewage poured into many basements, as well as some ground level floors. The water broke windows and destroyed carpets, walls, and personal belongings in many of the homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred once again along the Paria River, with the river gauge readings peaking at 2625 cfs.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on June 5th. While the front helped produce scattered showers and thunderstorms, the main impact was strong gusty winds across much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cold front brought strong northwesterly winds to the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains, with peak wind gusts of 74 mph at Lakeside Mountain, 71 mph at Camel Back Mountain, 68 mph at Hat Island, 65 mph at the Utah Test and Training Range Thermal Treatment Unit sensor, and 61 mph at Diddle Knoll.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milford ASOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on June 5th. While the front helped produce scattered showers and thunderstorms, the main impact was strong gusty winds across much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt Lake and Tooele valleys recorded strong wind gusts both ahead of and behind the cold front. Peak wind gusts included 77 mph at Flight Park South, 75 mph at Vernon Hill, and 61 mph at South Mountain. Tree damage was reported in parts of the Salt Lake Valley, including a large cottonwood tree that was knocked over in Cottonwood Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many of the wildfires that began in late June continued into July, burning thousands of acres and causing millions of dollars in damage. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clay Springs Fire began on July 27th east of Delta, Utah, and was human-caused. Clay Springs was the largest fire of the year in the state of Utah, burning a total of 107,846 acres before it was 100% contained on July 21st. The fire forced the evacuation of nearly 1,000 people, including the entire communities of Oak City and Fool Creek. Damages from the fire included one cabin that was destroyed, minor damage to a few other residences, 3 city storage structures destroyed, and dozens of power poles damaged. Two firefighters were injured by the blaze. In addition to physical damages, fire suppression cost a total of $6.6 million. Note that this event began in June, and monetary property damages listed here are for the entire event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many of the wildfires that began in late June continued into July, burning thousands of acres and causing millions of dollars in damage. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rose Crest Fire began on July 29th in Herriman, Utah. The fire was started when a car was parked on a very dry field; the heat from the car ignited the grass, and the fire quickly spread into the residential neighborhood. The fire forced the evacuation of nearly 1,000 residences, and eventually burned 612 acres before it was 100% contained on July 2nd. 4 residences were destroyed, 8 other residences sustained moderate to heavy damage, and 30 non-residential buildings were destroyed. In addition to these property damages, the fire suppression cost a total of $540,000. Note that this event began in June, and monetary property damages listed on this report are for the entire month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the end of the first week of July, monsoon moisture moved into the state from the south. Thunderstorms developed in this moist airmass, producing the first flash floods in what would become a very active month for flash flooding. While this additional moisture did not end the wildfire season completely, it reduced the explosive fire behavior that had been observed in the last week of June and first week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the Subway, a popular slot canyon in Zion National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture interacted with a strong upper-level low on September 11th, producing heavy rain and flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. The most significant event of the day was the failure of the Laub Dike in Santa Clara, causing flooding in many area homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Stout Creek. This flooding damaged a fly fishing operation along the creek, including filling a pond with silt, damaging a small bridge, and causing some erosion damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many of the wildfires that began in late June continued into July, burning thousands of acres and causing millions of dollars in damage. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seeley Fire began on June 23rd in the Manti-La Sal National Forest, and was caused by lightning. This fire burned for nearly a month, consuming 48,050 acres before it was 100% contained on July 18th. The fire closed several campgrounds and youth camps, shut down a major oil and gas field, shut down several working coal mines, and caused the evacuation of approximately 250 residences. Two non-residential buildings were damaged in the fire. In addition to the property damages, fire suppression efforts on the fire cost a total of $8.7 million. Note that this event began in June, and monetary property damages listed here are for the entire event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the end of the first week of July, monsoon moisture moved into the state from the south. Thunderstorms developed in this moist airmass, producing the first flash floods in what would become a very active month for flash flooding. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred on the Seeley burn scar following heavy rains. The flooding originated from near Engineer Canyon, and crossed State Route 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture interacted with a strong upper-level low on September 11th, producing heavy rain and flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. The most significant event of the day was the failure of the Laub Dike in Santa Clara, causing flooding in many area homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of moderate to heavy rainfall occurred in southwest Utah on the morning of September 11th, during which 3.24 inches of rainfall was measured in Ivins, Utah. In nearby Santa Clara, an earthen dike (called the Laub Dike) burst, releasing all of the water in the associated retention pond. This water and mud flowed into nearby homes and businesses, damaging both public infrastructure and private properties. A total of $3.8 million in damage was done to public infrastructure, including damage to the dike, streets, and sidewalks. The flood also damaged 16 businesses and 63 homes; some of these buildings had internal damage due to water and mud flowing into them, while other buildings experienced serious structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Baboon Fire began on July 12th in southwest Utah, and burned for about half of the month. The fire was named after nearby Baboon Peak. This fire caused evacuations and destroyed a couple of buildings before it became fully contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Baboon Fire began on July 12th, five miles southwest of Minersville, Utah, and was caused by lightning. The fire was originally thought to be 100% contained on July 16th, but began to spread again later in the month, when it was truly 100% contained on July 27th. This second flare-up caused no additional structural damage, and burned an additional 1,700 acres, bringing the total acreage consumed by the fire to 20,732 acres. This fire threatened the Circle Four Hog Farm, a very large farm with hundreds of thousands of pigs. Employees were evacuated from the farm for a short period of time, but the pigs were not evacuated. State Route 130 was also closed for about a day due to the fire. Two non-residential buildings were destroyed in the fire, one of them being a commercial building. In addition to these property damages, fire suppression costs totaled $650,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Church Camp Fire began on June 24th, approximately 22 miles south of Duchesne, Utah. The cause of the fire is unknown, but it was believed to be human-caused. The fire caused the evacuation of Argyle Canyon, a popular summer cabin and recreation area. A total of 7,211 acres were burned before the fire was 100% contained on July 7th. Three injuries to firefighters were reported, with one back injury and two heat-related injuries. The fire destroyed 15 cabins and 78 other non-residential buildings. In addition to the property damage, fire suppression costs totaled $12.2 million. Note that this event began in June, and monetary property damage listed here is for the entire event.

In total, the Shingle Fire burned 8,061 acres before it was 100% contained on July 11th. Four firefighters were injured while fighting the blaze. The fire caused the evacuation of approximately 500 structures, and ultimately destroyed one residence and one non-residential building. In addition to these property damages, fire suppression costs totaled $7.1 million.

The rain overwhelmed many of the storm drains across the city, with manhole covers popping up in a few locations, including Main Street. Water rose as high as three or four feet on some streets, and several drivers were stranded in their cars with water up to their windows. A few small landslides were reported, and mud and debris flowed onto some streets. Approximately 100 homes and a few businesses experienced at least minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many of the wildfires that began in late June continued into July, burning thousands of acres and causing millions of dollars in damage. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Quail Fire began in the foothills above Alpine, Utah on July 3rd, and was likely caused by a construction crew operating a back hoe. The fire burned 2,222 acres before it was 100% contained on July 10th. Multiple subdivisions in the Alpine area, comprised of more than 500 homes, were evacuated due to the fire. The fire destroyed one large barn and damaged five residences. In addition to these property damages, fire suppression costs totaled $1.5 million. This fire is not to be confused with another Quail Fire that occurred in June near Leeds, Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms remained very active across Utah at the end of the month of July. Several flash floods occurred during this period of time, and severe winds were observed in Utah's northwest desert on July 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris flow occurred at the Wood Hollow burn scar. This debris flow damaged multiple homes, with two homes in particular receiving significant damage. The main damage was caused by mud flowing into basements, with one home having muddy water pooled as much as 8 feet deep in the basement. In addition, the debris crossed over State Route 132 and closed the road for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many of the wildfires that began in late June continued into July, burning thousands of acres and causing millions of dollars in damage. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wood Hollow Fire began on June 23rd near Fountain Green, Utah in Sanpete County. The fire was ignited when gusty winds caused a power line to come too close to the top of another power pole, sparking a grass fire at its base. The electrical ground wires had been removed by copper thieves, which eliminated the system's ability to properly dissipate the electrical current. This fire caused one fatality, a 60-year-old male whose body was found inside a burned car. Two firefighters also sustained minor injuries. In total, the Wood Hollow Fire burned 47,387 acres, and caused extensive property damage. The communities of Indian Ridge, Elk Ridge, Big Hollow, and Oaker Hills all experienced mandatory evacuations. 52 residential buildings were destroyed, 108 other non-residential buildings were destroyed, and there was damage to local power infrastructure and the Indian Ridge water system. Some livestock was lost, with approximately 300 sheep killed and approximately 15 horses killed. In addition to these losses, fire suppression costs totaled $5.8 million. Note that this fire began in June, and the monetary property damages listed here are from the entire length of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level moisture over Utah helped produce increasingly organized convection beginning on July 10th. However, with the boundary layer remaining somewhat dry, these thunderstorms primarily produced strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 63 mph was measured at the Utah Test and Training Range, and windows were damaged on 7 of the cars in the parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon moisture in place at the end of July carried over into August, producing widespread showers and thunderstorms across Utah. The heaviest rain fell over southern Utah, where a few different flash floods were observed on August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After approximately 1.25 inches of rainfall, a very large debris flow occurred on the Seeley Burn Scar. Debris flowed over State Route 31, causing significant damage to a 1/4 mile stretch of the road, as well as a nearby bridge. Debris of up to 3 feet in height was spread over about a mile of the roadway. State Route 31 was closed for over a week, as crews repaired the road damage and cleared boulders and other debris off the roadway. The debris flow also pushed a UDOT front loader off the road and into a ditch, partially covering it with debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms continued across Utah in the middle of August. The strongest of these thunderstorms most commonly produced flash flooding in southern Utah, but isolated occurrences of severe winds and a tornado were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large flash flood occurred in the Subway portion of Zion National Park, as well as a few other surrounding drainages. Due to this flood, 22 people in 6 different groups were stranded in the Subway overnight. Two additional people were stranded overnight in Behunin Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon moisture in place at the end of July carried over into August, producing widespread showers and thunderstorms across Utah. The heaviest rain fell over southern Utah, where a few different flash floods were observed on August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along La Verkin Creek on the evening of August 1st, with significant rises in the water level. At the same time, a 2-year-old girl was noticed missing from her home in the city of La Verkin, which was close to the creek. The girl passed away when she drowned in the Creek, and her body was found a couple hours after she had been reported missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred across primarily northern Utah at the end of the month, producing strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Camel Back Mountain sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pinyon Fire was started by lightning on August 5th, and burned for a week. The fire started on the property of Army Garrison Camp Williams in Utah County, and primarily burned areas within that National Guard training site, but also threatened nearby neighborhoods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pinyon Fire began on Pinyon Hill on the afternoon of August 5th, and was caused by lightning. Pinyon Hill is located on the property of Army Garrison Camp Williams, a National Guard training site, and the fire largely burned within the confines of Camp Williams. The Pinyon Fire caused evacuations of about 100 homes in Eagle Mountain, and also jumped containment lines at one point to threaten parts of Herriman. Approximately 5,771 acres were burned before the fire was 100% contained on August 12th. The only major structural damage occurred at Camp Williams, where a mock Afghan village used for training purposes was damaged. The village had been built in 2010 at a total cost of $360,000, and 9 of the 15 buildings were damaged by the fire. In addition, fire suppression costs totaled $200,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms continued across Utah in the middle of August. The strongest of these thunderstorms most commonly produced flash flooding in southern Utah, but isolated occurrences of severe winds and a tornado were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an estimated 1.25 inches of rain fell over Zion National Park, flash flooding was reported in Spry Canyon, Pine Creek Canyon, and Echo Canyon. The heavy rainfall also caused rocks to fall onto State Route 9, between the switchbacks and the tunnel, which impacted traffic through the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across southern Utah once again reached a peak on August 22nd through 24th, with multiple flash floods reported each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported near Sheets Gulch and surrounding areas, with water flowing across Notom-Bullfrog Road and other roads on the east side of Capitol Reef National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across southern Utah once again reached a peak on August 22nd through 24th, with multiple flash floods reported each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Johnson Canyon, with a park ranger in the area calling it an average-sized flash flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred across primarily northern Utah at the end of the month, producing strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large line of thunderstorms moved into far western Utah on the evening of August 31st, bringing severe wind gusts. Peak recorded gusts included 72 mph at Wendover Airport, 61 mph at Wendover Peak, and 58 mph at the North Salt Flats sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred across primarily northern Utah at the end of the month, producing strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified as it reached the Great Salt Lake, with peak recorded wind gusts of 78 mph at the I-80 at Grassey sensor, 67 mph at Lakeside Mountain, 65 mph at Hat Island, and 62 mph at the Eagle Range sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across southern Utah once again reached a peak on August 22nd through 24th, with multiple flash floods reported each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along the Paria River, with the river gauge reaching a peak observation of 3850 cfs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 2 inches fell over southern Washington County, causing flash flooding around parts of Hildale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms continued across Utah in the middle of August. The strongest of these thunderstorms most commonly produced flash flooding in southern Utah, but isolated occurrences of severe winds and a tornado were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large flash flood along the Paria River was observed as it passed under State Route 89. The spotter estimated that the first 200 to 300 yards of the flood was composed of debris, including trees and logs. The gauge along the river increased to 8200 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and unseasonably cold storm system moved into Utah beginning October 22. Strong southwesterly winds were observed ahead of the storm, and heavy snow was observed across primarily northern Utah. Progressively colder air moved into the state through the event, with heavy snow eventually reaching the valley floors of northern Utah and causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 3 inches of snow fell across the southern Wasatch Front with this storm system. Combined with the fact that most trees still had their leaves intact, many fallen tree branches were reported, causing power outages primarily in Lindon and Pleasant Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and unseasonably cold storm system moved into Utah beginning October 22. Strong southwesterly winds were observed ahead of the storm, and heavy snow was observed across primarily northern Utah. Progressively colder air moved into the state through the event, with heavy snow eventually reaching the valley floors of northern Utah and causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals in the Salt Lake and Tooele valleys included 7 inches of snow in Tooele and 6 inches in Upper Millcreek. Combined with the fact that most trees still had their leaves intact, several fallen tree branches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and unseasonably cold storm system moved into Utah beginning October 22. Strong southwesterly winds were observed ahead of the storm, and heavy snow was observed across primarily northern Utah. Progressively colder air moved into the state through the event, with heavy snow eventually reaching the valley floors of northern Utah and causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow levels reached the floor of the Cache Valley on the morning of October 23, much earlier in the storm than along the Wasatch Front. Generally 2 to 3 inches of snow fell across the Cache Valley through the storm. Combined with the fact that most trees still had their leaves intact, many fallen tree branches were reported, causing power outages to several hundred homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wolf Den wildfire was started by lightning on the afternoon of June 29th in a remote part of the Tavaputs plateau. This fire continued to burn through much of July before full containment on July 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wolf Den wildfire was a lightning caused fire that burned approximately 19,800 acres across the remote Tavaputs Plateau.  Oil and gas infrastructure, cultural and historic resources, deer and elk winter range, and sage grouse habitat were at risk. The only structure destroyed was an historic cabin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture that moved into Utah at the end of August remained in place for the beginning of September. This moist airmass produced widespread showers and thunderstorms, with several of them becoming severe or producing flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Clay Springs burn scar caused a debris flow on the afternoon of September 1st. Four homes in the Fools Creek area experienced flooded basements, and one yard was severely damaged. In addition, a single wide trailer in the same area was pushed off its foundation. Six to eight inches of mud and debris crossed Highway 125, causing some damage along the edges of the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture that moved into Utah at the end of August remained in place for the beginning of September. This moist airmass produced widespread showers and thunderstorms, with several of them becoming severe or producing flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Dump Fire burn scar created a debris flow, flooding a portion of Saratoga Springs, and forcing the closure of Highway 68 for a period of time. Twenty residences experienced flood damage, with 11 of those homes having water in their basements, and 9 of those homes experiencing solely yard damage. Finished basements were completely destroyed in 5 separate homes, with the most impacted home having mud nine feet deep in the basement. Structural damage to homes was estimated at $250,000, and many personal possessions inside the homes were also damaged or destroyed. Public infrastructure was also impacted, with approximately $200,000 worth of damage to a nearby park and $50,000 of fencing destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into Utah on October 16, bringing a significantly colder airmass into the state. The main impact of this storm was the strong winds observed across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty west-northwesterly winds were observed across the northern Wasatch Front, with a peak observed wind gust of 51 mph at both Bothwell and Washington Terrace. In addition, an empty semitrailer was blown over on Interstate 15 near Brigham City, briefly causing traffic issues on the northbound thoroughfare.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large closed low passed through southern Utah on October 12. This low produced widespread rain across the state, and caused flooding in parts of southeast Utah, including Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Capitol Reef National Park, with all washes along Scenic Drive producing 6 to 8 inch flows across the road. This flooding caused many of the dirt roads across the park to be impassable for a few days. In addition, a large tree fell over State Route 24 due to erosion along the Fremont River, closing the highway for about an hour and a half. Water also crossed over State Route 24 at milepost 100 near Caineville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and unseasonably cold storm system moved into Utah beginning October 22. Strong southwesterly winds were observed ahead of the storm, and heavy snow was observed across primarily northern Utah. Progressively colder air moved into the state through the event, with heavy snow eventually reaching the valley floors of northern Utah and causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wasatch Mountains north of I-80 had the largest snow totals in Utah from this storm. Much of the snow fell early in the event, with Randolph receiving 9 inches of snow in the first 12 hours of the event, and Monte Cristo receiving 26 inches of snow in the first 24 hours of the event. Eventual storm totals included 42 inches of snow at Powder Mountain, 30 inches at Monte Cristo, and 17 inches of snow at both Randolph and Laketown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred across the Ogden Valley, with storm totals of 28 inches of snow in Liberty and 21 inches of snow in Eden. The early snowfall also impacted other parts of the Wasatch mountain valleys, with school being cancelled at Mountain Green Elementary and Morgan Elementary, and downed trees causing power outages in multiple communities, including Kamas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on June 5th. While the front helped produce scattered showers and thunderstorms, the main impact was strong gusty winds across much of the state.

In addition, a semitrailer was knocked over on northbound I-15 near Lehi, and a couple of medium sized trees were toppled or broken in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With very dry fuels, a very dry air mass, and an increasing southwesterly flow as a low pressure system remained near the Pacific Coast, conditions across Utah became perfect for explosive fire growth. The winds were severe at times across northwest Utah, and any fires that started spread very quickly. Several of these wildfires caused property damage or injuries, and one caused a fatality. Note that some of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged windy conditions existed across the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains, as a low near the west coast kept the area in persistent southwesterly flow. Peak wind gusts included 60 mph at Durand Road, 59 mph at Upper Cedar Mountain, and 58 mph at the Ditto sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With very dry fuels, a very dry air mass, and an increasing southwesterly flow as a low pressure system remained near the Pacific Coast, conditions across Utah became perfect for explosive fire growth. The winds were severe at times across northwest Utah, and any fires that started spread very quickly. Several of these wildfires caused property damage or injuries, and one caused a fatality. Note that some of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a power line in West Jordan, Utah, igniting a field. This fire quickly spread into nearby homes, burning two houses. In addition, a third home suffered significant smoke damage, and the fire damaged a fence and a gazebo. One firefighter suffered heat exhaustion while battling the blaze, which was put out later that day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire started in a field in Draper, Utah, damaging one home and threatening two others. The fire burned through 150 feet of wooden fencing, and caused light damage to a garage and a vehicle at one of the homes, before being contained 25 minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With very dry fuels, a very dry air mass, and an increasing southwesterly flow as a low pressure system remained near the Pacific Coast, conditions across Utah became perfect for explosive fire growth. The winds were severe at times across northwest Utah, and any fires that started spread very quickly. Several of these wildfires caused property damage or injuries, and one caused a fatality. Note that some of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Quail Fire began near Leeds, Utah on June 23rd, and was ignited by people burning debris. The fire threatened some residences in the area, with approximately 60 homes being evacuated, and also threatened a desert tortoise habitat. Two sheds were destroyed in the fire, local power infrastructure was damaged, and one firefighter suffered a heat-related injury. The fire burned 1,708 acres before being 100% contained on June 25th, and fire suppression cost a total of $250,000. This fire is not to be confused with another Quail Fire that began in July near Alpine, Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With very dry fuels, a very dry air mass, and an increasing southwesterly flow as a low pressure system remained near the Pacific Coast, conditions across Utah became perfect for explosive fire growth. The winds were severe at times across northwest Utah, and any fires that started spread very quickly. Several of these wildfires caused property damage or injuries, and one caused a fatality. Note that some of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seeley Fire began on June 23rd in the Manti-La Sal National Forest, and was caused by lightning. This fire burned for nearly a month, consuming 48,050 acres before it was 100% contained on July 18th. The fire closed several campgrounds and youth camps, shut down a major oil and gas field, shut down several working coal mines, and caused the evacuation of approximately 250 residences. Two non-residential buildings were damaged in the fire. In addition to the property damages, fire suppression efforts on the fire cost a total of $8.7 million. Note that this event continued into July, and monetary property damages will be listed on the July report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With very dry fuels, a very dry air mass, and an increasing southwesterly flow as a low pressure system remained near the Pacific Coast, conditions across Utah became perfect for explosive fire growth. The winds were severe at times across northwest Utah, and any fires that started spread very quickly. Several of these wildfires caused property damage or injuries, and one caused a fatality. Note that some of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Church Camp Fire began on June 24th, approximately 22 miles south of Duchesne, Utah. The cause of the fire is unknown, but it was believed to be human-caused. The fire caused the evacuation of Argyle Canyon, a popular summer cabin and recreation area. A total of 7,211 acres were burned before the fire was 100% contained on July 7th. Three injuries to firefighters were reported, with one back injury and two heat-related injuries. The fire destroyed 15 cabins and 78 other non-residential buildings. In addition to the property damage, fire suppression costs totaled $12.2 million. Note that this event continued into July, and monetary property damage totals will be listed on the July report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With very dry fuels, a very dry air mass, and an increasing southwesterly flow as a low pressure system remained near the Pacific Coast, conditions across Utah became perfect for explosive fire growth. The winds were severe at times across northwest Utah, and any fires that started spread very quickly. Several of these wildfires caused property damage or injuries, and one caused a fatality. Note that some of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dump Fire began on June 21st on the west side of Utah Lake, and was caused by target shooters using explosive targets. This fire threatened the communities of Saratoga Springs and Eagle Mountain, leading to the evacuation of nearly 600 homes and 2,500 people beginning on June 22nd. Fire suppression was able to keep the fire from reaching the communities, and the evacuations were lifted by the end of June 23rd. The fire was 100% contained on June 25th, after it had burned a total of 5,507 acres. Two firefighters were injured; one received a first degree burn, and the other became dehydrated and required transport to a local hospital. The only reported property damage was power line infrastructure damaged in the fire. In addition to this damage, fire suppression costs totaled $2.1 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of eastern Utah by the end of May. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions expanded into all of southeast Utah in May as precipitation remained well below normal.

Note that some of these fires continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wood Hollow Fire began on June 23rd near Fountain Green, Utah in Sanpete County. The fire was ignited when gusty winds caused a power line to come too close to the top of another power pole, sparking a grass fire at its base. The electrical ground wires had been removed by copper thieves, which eliminated the system's ability to properly dissipate the electrical current. This fire caused one fatality, a 60-year-old male whose body was found inside a burned car. Two firefighters also sustained minor injuries.||In total, the Wood Hollow Fire burned 47,387 acres, and caused extensive property damage. The communities of Indian Ridge, Elk Ridge, Big Hollow, and Oaker Hills all experienced mandatory evacuations.  52 residential buildings were destroyed, 108 other non-residential buildings were destroyed, and there was damage to local power infrastructure and the Indian Ridge water system. Some livestock was lost, with approximately 300 sheep killed and approximately 15 horses killed. In addition to these losses, fire suppression costs totaled $5.8 million. Note that this fire continued into July, and the monetary property damages will be listed in July's report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms remained very active across Utah at the end of the month of July. Several flash floods occurred during this period of time, and severe winds were observed in Utah's northwest desert on July 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris flow occurred on the Seeley burn scar, originating from canyons of Nuc Woodward, South Hughes, and Chute, with debris flowing onto State Route 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep Pacific moisture, combined with embedded weather disturbances moving through the region in the middle of January, resulted in heavy and wet snowfall in the mountains and mountain valleys of northern Utah. Occasional heavy rain was observed in the northern Utah valleys. This rain, when combined with frozen ground and snowmelt, produced flooding in portions of Cache Valley and Ogden Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in the Cache Valley, combined with frozen ground and snowmelt, produced flooding in portions of Mendon and Wellsville. Culverts in those areas quickly became clogged with debris, and several homes reported basement flooding. State Route 23 was also closed for a period of time due to water flowing across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level moisture over Utah helped produce increasingly organized convection beginning on July 10th. However, with the boundary layer remaining somewhat dry, these thunderstorms primarily produced strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ogden Peak sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across southern Utah once again reached a peak on August 22nd through 24th, with multiple flash floods reported each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Buckskin Gulch, blocking the route to The Wave, a popular hiking area.
